does: This paper investigates whether behavioral tendencies in language models amplify or decay when models are iteratively finetuned on their own outputs. The authors train sequences of models across three settings: supervised finetuning on instruction-tuned models, synthetic document finetuning on base models, and direct preference optimization. They seed initial models with specific personas or beliefs and track how these traits evolve across generations. The main finding is that traits in SFT and SDF settings mostly remain stable or decay, with iterative finetuning having minimal effect—a property they term idempotent behavior. Amplification is rare and typically degrades model coherence. In DPO settings, amplification occurs only with continual post-training preference learning on the model's own outputs, but disappears with model reinitialization between cycles.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns in AI safety about distributional shift and self-reinforcing behavioral drift in language models. It relates to research on model alignment failures, sycophancy, and how training on model-generated data affects learning dynamics—issues studied in work on synthetic data scaling and preference learning vulnerabilities. The paper engages with questions about preference optimization methods and their stability properties, extending prior analyses of how DPO and other RL-based approaches respond to repeated application. It also connects to foundational work on the effects of training on model outputs, building on observations about data poisoning and feedback loops in iterative learning systems.
enables: These findings suggest new directions for understanding model safety and training robustness. The idempotency property in non-RL finetuning could inform defenses against trait amplification by limiting continual post-training stages in deployment pipelines. The observation that amplification requires specific conditions—particularly continual preference learning—points toward mechanisms for controlling behavioral drift and suggests design principles for safer iterative training. Future work could investigate the theoretical foundations of why certain training regimes exhibit idempotent behavior while others amplify tendencies, explore boundary conditions where amplification becomes significant, and develop interventions that preserve beneficial properties while preventing undesired trait escalation in long-horizon model development.
